reasoning_summary: The fresh post-cleanup custom baseline is now 24.324 ms, and the fresh post-cleanup cuBLAS baseline is 22.000 ms. That leaves a clean ~2.32 ms gap to close. The good news is that the accepted PTX hot-band baseline remains stable after cleanup: registers are back at 200, active warps are 16.62, and the hot-band split is no longer contaminated by the earlier stale GPU process. The remaining gap still shows up in the same two local symptoms versus cuBLAS: barrier stalls are 5.46% instead of 0.16%, and long-scoreboard stalls are 7.24% instead of 0.18%. Because the staged 128x128x32 variant already looked structurally worse and the row-band expansion family was contaminated and is now excluded from ranking, the next clean round should stay on the accepted PTX hot-band path and test the cheapest launch-order lever first: retune kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows to reuse each hot-band B tile across a deeper row group. The second direction is the single-K non-microkernel 128x128 sibling, which keeps the same row split and stage depth while changing the accumulate ordering. The third direction is a launch-bounds retune, kept only as a fallback because cuBLAS does not suggest occupancy is the main missing ingredient.

family_audit.rationale: This is the cheapest clean perturbation on top of the accepted PTX hot-band baseline, and it directly targets B-tile reuse and scoreboard behavior without changing the hot-band row split or K-stage structure.
The existing non-microkernel sibling is still the cleanest kernel-variant experiment that preserves the accepted row split and single-K stage depth.
Launch-bounds tuning can still be explored, but the cuBLAS reference shows lower active-warps than the custom kernel, so occupancy is not the first thing to chase.

directions.name: Increase PTX Grouped-Row Depth On The Clean Baseline
directions.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hot-band baseline may still be wasting issue slots because each hot-band B tile is only reused across four adjacent row tiles before the traversal advances columns. Increasing kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 8 should keep each B tile live across more rows, potentially reducing scoreboard pressure without changing the row split or K-stage structure.
directions.expected_bottleneck: launch-order and B-tile reuse inefficiency inside the accepted PTX hot-band traversal
directions.risk: Low-to-moderate risk: the edit is isolated, but deeper row grouping can help B reuse while hurting A locality or cache balance.
ranking_notes: This is the fastest clean experiment to run on the accepted PTX hot-band baseline.
It keeps the search on the same family that currently produces the best custom runtime.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change stays within noise or if long-scoreboard stalls rise after the first clean measurement.
expected_local_changes: Keep the accepted PTX hot-band row split and default launch intact.
Increase kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 8 and remeasure on the same fixed benchmark.

directions.name: Swap To The Single-K 128x128 Non-Microkernel Sibling
directions.hypothesis: The existing single-K 128x128 sibling keeps the accepted row split and one-K stage depth while changing the accumulate ordering away from the microkernel-specific path. That may trim scoreboard waste without paying the staged-128x128x32 cost.
directions.expected_bottleneck: microkernel-specific accumulate ordering is contributing to scoreboard overhead on the accepted hot-band split
directions.risk: Moderate risk: the variant is in-tree, but it still lacks a clean measurement on the current baseline.
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest kernel-variant swap that stays on the accepted row split.
It ranks behind grouped-row tuning because the launch-order experiment is cheaper and more directly aligned with the scoreboard gap.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime regresses while register pressure and active-warps do not materially improve.
expected_local_changes: Keep the accepted PTX hot-band row split.
Swap the default hot-band launch from the PTX microkernel path to the existing single-K non-microkernel sibling.

directions.name: Retune PTX Launch Bounds As A Fallback
directions.hypothesis: The clean PTX hotspot still sits at 200 registers per thread with occupancy capped at two blocks per SM. Tightening __launch_bounds__ may trim register pressure enough to improve latency hiding if the compiler does not spill.
directions.expected_bottleneck: register pressure and low CTA residency on the accepted PTX hot-band path
directions.risk: High risk: forcing tighter launch bounds can introduce spills or a weaker schedule, and the cuBLAS reference does not point to occupancy as the main missing ingredient.
ranking_notes: This remains worth keeping because the PTX path is still latency-limited.
It ranks third because the clean cuBLAS profile does not suggest occupancy is the main missing lever.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if build output or NCU shows spills, or if runtime regresses without reducing register pressure.
expected_local_changes: Retune the PTX microkernel launch-bounds annotation and keep the rest of the launch topology unchanged for the first measurement.

notes: This diagnosis starts the fresh clean 10-round loop. The earlier contaminated absolute timings are excluded from ranking except as weak structural hints.
